name: Ningyo
description: The Ningyo, also known as the Japanese mermaid, is a creature from Japanese folklore. Typically depicted as having the head of a human and the body of a fish, the Ningyo's appearance can vary depending on the region and the specific story. Some accounts portray them as beautiful and alluring, while others describe them as grotesque and monstrous. The Ningyo's flesh is said to grant immortality to anyone who consumes it, although obtaining it often comes at a great cost or through tragic circumstances. The creature is often associated with misfortune, storms, and impending disasters, serving as an omen of bad luck. While some stories depict Ningyo as benevolent beings capable of healing or offering guidance, they are more commonly feared and respected for their connection to the unpredictable power of the ocean. Their tears are sometimes said to transform into pearls, a reflection of the preciousness and rarity of encountering such a creature. Despite their often negative portrayal, the Ningyo remains a captivating figure in Japanese mythology, embodying the mysteries and dangers of the sea.
subtitle: Japanese Mermaid of Immortality and Omen
appearance: The Ningyo typically possesses the head of a human, which can range from beautiful to grotesque, and the body of a fish, complete with scales and fins. The scales are often described as iridescent, shimmering with various colors. Variations exist, with some Ningyo having clawed hands in place of fins, or possessing sharp teeth and wild hair. Their eyes are often described as large and expressive, capable of conveying a range of emotions from sorrow to anger.
habitat: Ningyo primarily inhabit coastal waters, rivers, and lakes throughout Japan. They are often associated with deep, unexplored areas, where they remain hidden from human contact. Some stories suggest they can also be found in remote islands and secluded coves.
behavior: Ningyo are generally solitary creatures, rarely interacting with humans unless driven by necessity or a specific purpose. They are often portrayed as melancholic and lonely, lamenting their existence. Their behavior can be unpredictable, ranging from shy and elusive to aggressive and dangerous, especially when provoked or their territory is threatened. They are said to possess a deep understanding of the ocean's currents and tides.

keyMyths.title: Yao Bikuni
keyMyths.description: The most famous Ningyo myth tells the story of Yao Bikuni, a woman who lived for 800 years after accidentally consuming Ningyo flesh. Her extended life brought her both wisdom and sorrow, as she witnessed the rise and fall of generations.

keyMyths.title: Ningyo's Tears
keyMyths.description: Some tales claim that a Ningyo's tears turn into pearls. This highlights the rarity and value associated with these creatures, and the potential beauty that can arise from sadness.

sources.author: Lafcadio Hearn
sources.work: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things
sources.description: A collection of Japanese ghost stories and folklore, possibly including mentions of Ningyo.
sources.text: A collection of Japanese ghost stories and folklore, possibly including mentions of Ningyo.

sources.author: Shigeru Mizuki
sources.description: Mizuki's yokai encyclopedia and manga feature many Japanese monsters, potentially including Ningyo.
